examples.title: Process Creation using fork()
examples.description: Demonstrates how to create a child process in Unix/Linux using fork() system call
examples.explanation: The fork() system call duplicates the current process. It returns twice: once in parent (returns child's PID) and once in child (returns 0). Parent uses wait() to wait for child's termination.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for fork, parent wait depends on child
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for process creation overhead, child gets copy of parent's address space

examples.title: Process Scheduling Simulation
examples.description: Simulates basic process states and transitions in an operating system
examples.code:
from enum import Enum
from collections import deque
import random
import time

class ProcessState(Enum):
    NEW = "New"
    READY = "Ready"
    RUNNING = "Running"
    BLOCKED = "Blocked"
    TERMINATED = "Terminated"

class Process:
    def __init__(self, pid, name):
        self.pid = pid
        self.name = name
        self.state = ProcessState.NEW
        self.ProgramCounter = 0
        
    def __str__(self):
        return f"Process {self.pid} ({self.name}): {self.state.value}"

class Scheduler:
    def __init__(self, time_quantum=3):
        self.ready_queue = deque()
        self.time_quantum = time_quantum
        
    def admit(self, process):
        process.state = ProcessState.READY
        self.ready_queue.append(process)
        print(f"Admitted: {process}")
    
    def schedule(self):
        if not self.ready_queue:
            print("No processes in ready queue")
            return None
        
        process = self.ready_queue.popleft()
        process.state = ProcessState.RUNNING
        print(f"Running: {process}")
        
        # Simulate execution
        for i in range(self.time_quantum):
            process.ProgramCounter += 1
            if random.random() < 0.3:  # 30% chance of I/O
                process.state = ProcessState.BLOCKED
                print(f"Process {process.pid} blocked (I/O wait)")
                return process
        
        # Time quantum exhausted - back to ready
        process.state = ProcessState.READY
        self.ready_queue.append(process)
        print(f"Time quantum expired: {process}")
        return process

# Demonstration
scheduler = Scheduler(time_quantum=3)
processes = [Process(i, f"P{i}") for i in range(1, 4)]

for p in processes:
    scheduler.admit(p)

print("\n--- Scheduling Simulation ---\n")
for _ in range(5):
    current = scheduler.schedule()
    time.sleep(0.5)
examples.explanation: This simulation shows process state transitions: NEW→READY (admission), READY→RUNNING (scheduling), RUNNING→READY (time quantum exhausted), RUNNING→BLOCKED (I/O wait). The scheduler implements basic round-robin behavior.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for each scheduling decision with deque
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of processes in ready queue

examples.title: PCB Structure Implementation
examples.description: Implementation of Process Control Block structure in C
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the structure of a Process Control Block (PCB) in C. It includes all essential fields: PID, parent PID, process state, CPU registers, memory info, scheduling parameters, I/O status, and accounting data. The PCB is the fundamental data structure used by the OS to manage processes.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) per PCB, total O(n) for n processes
